title: Building Proactive Relationships and Avoiding Grievances
learningObjectives: Identify the most common accessibility-related complaints and how to prevent them
Build a proactive working relationship with your Disability Services office before problems arise
Include accessibility information in your syllabus and first-day-of-class communication
Know when to handle an issue yourself vs. refer to Disability Services

keyTerms.definition: A clear, welcoming statement in your syllabus that (1) tells students how to request accommodations, (2) provides the contact information for Disability Services, and (3) commits to working with them. Sets expectations early and reduces last-minute scrambles.

A formal claim by a student that an institution has violated their rights under the ADA or Section 504. Can be filed with the institution's internal grievance process or with the U.S. Department of Education's Office for Civil Rights (OCR). Complaints are serious and time-consuming.

A formal complaint filed with the U.S. Department of Education, alleging disability discrimination. OCR investigates and can require an institution to pay damages, provide compensatory education, or enter a resolution agreement with monitoring. These are public record.

Proactive communication from the faculty member (or institution) to students with disabilities, typically at the start of the semester: 'If you need accommodations, here is how to request them; here is my contact info; I am here to support you.' This removes barriers and prevents late surprises.

An ongoing working relationship where you consult with Disability Services before problems arise. You know who to call, they know your course, and you can solve problems together rather than defensively.

contentBlocks.title: The Top 5 Accessibility Complaints (and How to Prevent Them)
contentBlocks.body:
According to OCR resolution agreements and complaint data, here are the accessibility complaints that appear most often in higher ed. The good news: all of them are preventable.

**1. Late Implementation of Accommodations**

*The complaint:* "I gave my professor an accommodation letter in week 2. It is now week 5, and they still have not implemented extended time on any quizzes. They say they will start 'next quiz' — but that might not be for another two weeks." By the time the accommodation is actually in place, the student has already taken multiple assessments without it, affecting their grade.

*Prevention:* Implement accommodations by the next assessment after you receive the letter — not "eventually." If you get an accommodation letter on a Wednesday, and a quiz is due Friday, it might be too late for that quiz, but you should reach out to the student: "I got your accommodation letter. Extended time starts on the next quiz, Monday." And make it happen. Have a system: put accommodation letters in a folder, check it before every quiz, and verify accommodations are set.

**2. Inaccessible Course Materials Without Alternatives**

*The complaint:* "My syllabus is a scanned image PDF. My professor posted it on the LMS. I asked for an alternative format. My professor said, 'It is readable enough.' I cannot copy/paste it into my screen reader, and I cannot search it. I have been in the course for three weeks without an accessible syllabus. This is discrimination."

*Prevention:* Every document you post — syllabus, readings, handouts, slides — should be natively accessible (born-digital PDFs with tags, Word docs, etc.) or immediately available in an alternative format. If you have a scanned document, provide it in an editable format or run it through your institution's Document Ally Pro or similar. Do this *before* the student asks. Not "we can get you an alternative if you ask." Just provide it.

**3. Faculty Questioning or Dismissing Accommodations**

*The complaint:* "My professor said, 'extended time is unfair — everyone else has to finish on time.' They also asked me 'what is your disability?' I felt blamed and humiliated. I dropped the course."

*Prevention:* Never question the validity of an accommodation, never suggest a student is 'trying to cheat,' and never ask about the diagnosis. When you receive an accommodation letter, your response is: "Thank you. I will implement this." Not "Are you sure you need this?" Not "I am not sure this is appropriate for my course." The DS office has already vetted the accommodation. Your job is to implement it professionally and confidentially.

**4. Public Identification of a Student with a Disability**

*The complaint:* "My professor announced to the class, 'This student has a disability so they get extended time.' Now my classmates know, and I have faced stigma. I also did not want my disability disclosed." Or: "My professor sent an email to the class saying, 'I have a student with an accommodation, so I am changing the quiz format for everyone.' Now everyone knows I have an accommodation."

*Prevention:* Implement accommodations silently. Do not announce them to the class. Do not email "I have a student with an accommodation so..." Instead, you quietly add extended time to that student's quiz. You quietly accept a note-taker in class. You quietly send an accessible PDF to one student. No announcements. No class-wide changes to benefit the one student (though universal design for learning might naturally benefit everyone — that is different).

**5. Inflexible Attendance/Participation Policies**

*The complaint:* "My professor has a 'no absences except for medical emergency' policy. I have a chronic illness and need flexibility. I asked for accommodations. My professor said, 'Accommodations do not override attendance policy.' I lost points for absences related to my disability. This is discrimination."

*Prevention:* Build flexibility into your attendance policy from the start. Something like: "Attendance is expected. If you have recurring absences due to medical or accessibility needs, contact Disability Services. We will work together on a plan." This signals that you understand disabilities can affect attendance and you are willing to problem-solve. It also signals that outright absences are still accountable — you are not saying "skip whenever you want." You are saying "let's talk."

**Prevention strategy:** All five of these complaints stem from the same root: faculty not implementing accommodations promptly, confidentially, professionally, and without question. If you receive an accommodation letter, implement it the way you would implement any institutional policy — promptly and without debate.

contentBlocks.title: Your Accessibility Statement and First-Day Practices
contentBlocks.body:
Prevention starts before anyone needs an accommodation. Here is what to include in your syllabus and how to frame it on the first day of class.

**Syllabus Statement:**

Include something like this:

> *Accessibility and Accommodations*
>
> *I am committed to creating an accessible learning environment for all students. The [Institution] Office of Disability Services (or Accessibility Services) determines accommodations for students with documented disabilities.*
>
> *If you think you may have a disability (visible or invisible) and might benefit from accommodations, or if you are already registered with Disability Services, please reach out to them at [phone/email/website]. They will work with you to determine appropriate accommodations, and will send me an accommodation letter.*
>
> *I will implement all accommodations listed in the accommodation letter. If you have questions about your accommodations, or if you believe accommodations are not being implemented, please let me know or contact Disability Services.*
>
> *Additionally, I aim to build this course with Universal Design for Learning (UDL) principles in mind — multiple means of representation, action/expression, and engagement. This benefits all learners.*
>
> *For contact information:*
> *Disability Services: [phone] [email] [office location]*
> *My office hours: [times and location]*

This statement is warm, clear, and pro-active. It signals that accommodations are normal, expected, and confidential. It also mentions UDL so students know you are thinking about accessibility broadly, not just retroactively.

**First-Day Practices:**

On the first day of class (or in the first email for an online course):

1. **Mention the accessibility statement.** "I have an accessibility statement in the syllabus. If you need accommodations, here is how to request them. I take this seriously."

2. **Normalize disability without calling students out.** "Many students have accommodations, and that is completely normal. You do not have to disclose your disability to me — Disability Services handles that confidentially."

3. **Give clear contact info.** "Here is my email. Here is the Disability Services contact." Make it easy for students to reach you or DS.

4. **Commit to confidentiality.** "If you do reach out about accessibility, it is between us." This is reassuring.

5. **Set the tone that access is not charity, it is right.** Your language should be: "Accommodations level the playing field so you can demonstrate what you know." Not: "We will do our best to help you." Not: "I will try to make some changes." You are not offering a favor; you are ensuring access to an equal education.

**Why early outreach matters:**

When a student hears these messages on day one, they are more likely to self-disclose to Disability Services early (not week 10). When they request accommodations early, you have time to set up extended time in the LMS, coordinate with a note-taker, and order alternative materials. When everything is set *before* the first quiz or assignment, there are no crises. The student can focus on learning instead of fighting for access.

contentBlocks.title: The Escalation Decision Tree
contentBlocks.body:
One of the trickiest situations is knowing when to handle an accessibility issue yourself and when to refer it to Disability Services. Here is a practical decision tree.

**Scenario 1: "I cannot navigate your syllabus with my screen reader."**

*Can I handle this?* Yes. This is a document-level accessibility issue. You learned how to fix this in Module 3 (headings, alt text, links). You can fix the syllabus structure and re-upload it.

*Action:* Fix the syllabus. Email the student: "I fixed the document structure. Please try again and let me know if you encounter any more issues."

**Scenario 2: "I need extended time on exams."**

*Can I handle this?* Partially. If the student already has an accommodation letter, you configure the extended time in Canvas/Blackboard. But the student needs to get the accommodation letter from Disability Services first.

*Action:* If the student doesn't have a letter yet: "Please reach out to Disability Services [contact info] to request accommodations. Once they send me an accommodation letter, I can set up extended time in our quiz system."

If the student has a letter: Set up extended time in Canvas immediately.

**Scenario 3: "I need a note-taker because I have trouble taking notes."**

*Can I handle this?* No. You cannot be the note-taker (that is a conflict of interest), and students need to go through Disability Services to be matched with a trained volunteer note-taker.

*Action:* "Please contact Disability Services at [contact info]. They will match you with a note-taker and let me know when they are starting. My role is to help ensure the note-taker has access to slides and course materials."

**Scenario 4: "I need a sign language interpreter for the lecture."**

*Can I handle this?* No. Interpreters are expensive, must be professional, and require coordination. This is a Disability Services role.

*Action:* "Please contact Disability Services. They have a process for arranging and paying for interpreters." Do not try to find an interpreter yourself or tell the student to hire one.

**Scenario 5: "I don't think the textbook is accessible."**

*Can I handle this?* Partially. If it is a scanned, inaccessible PDF, you can contact your library's Accessibility Services team (many institutions have this). But coordinating alternative format requests is typically done through DS and the library.

*Action:* "Let me check with our library and Disability Services to see if we can get this in an accessible format. I will also look for an OER alternative." Then follow up through the proper channels.

**Scenario 6: "This quiz is not working right for my screen reader."**

*Can I handle this?* Probably yes. This is a technical accessibility issue with your quiz, not a disability services issue. You may need technical support from your LMS help desk.

*Action:* "I want to fix this. Can you tell me which question type is causing the problem? I can also contact Canvas support to troubleshoot." Then use Module 6 knowledge to fix the quiz design.

**Scenario 7: "I disagreed with the accommodation the DS office assigned, but my professor implemented it anyway. I think it was unfair."**

*Can I handle this?* Not really. This is a dispute between the student and DS about what accommodation is appropriate. You, as the faculty member, should not be in the middle of this conflict. Your role is to implement what the accommodation letter says, not to referee disputes about what the letter should say.

*Action:* If a student expresses disagreement with their accommodation, direct them to DS: "If you think your accommodation doesn't fit your needs, please talk to Disability Services. They may be able to adjust it. I will implement whatever the updated letter says."

**General principle:**

You handle things that fall within your expertise (document structure, course design, quiz setup). You refer things that require expert DS knowledge (determining disability status, arranging interpreters, coordinating professional services, or resolving disputes about what accommodations should be). When in doubt, email your DS office and ask. They will tell you.

contentBlocks.title: Accommodation Escalation Decision Tree
contentBlocks.imageAlt: Flowchart for handling accommodation requests. One: is the accommodation documented in the DRC letter? If no, contact DRC for documentation. Two: can you implement it yourself? If no, contact DRC for guidance. Three: does it conflict with an essential course function? If yes, work with DRC on alternatives. If no, implement within 48 hours and confirm in writing with the student.
contentBlocks.imageCaption: When in doubt, loop in DRC early — they answer fast and protect you legally.

contentBlocks.body: Prevent complaints by implementing accommodations promptly and confidentially, building accessibility into your syllabus from the start, treating accommodations as standard practice (not charity), and knowing when to handle issues yourself versus when to escalate to Disability Services. Build a proactive relationship with your DS office before problems arise — know who to call, include their contact in your syllabus, and reach out early with questions.

freeTextPrompt.prompt: Review your current syllabus. Does it include an accessibility statement? Does it tell students how to request accommodations and provide the Disability Services contact information? If yes, evaluate it against the guidance in this section — is it welcoming and clear? If no, draft a 3–4 sentence accessibility statement you would add to your syllabus.
freeTextPrompt.rubric: A strong response shows the faculty member actually reviewed their syllabus. If they have an accessibility statement, they evaluate it using the criteria from the content (clarity, warmth, confidentiality signal, DS contact info). If they don't have one, they draft a statement that includes: (1) a signal that accommodations are normal/expected, (2) how to contact Disability Services, (3) the faculty member's commitment to implementing accommodations, and (4) tone that is welcoming, not defensive. The statement is practical and could be copy-pasted directly into a syllabus.
